event: Expulsion of the Money Changers from the Temple
symbols: figure in white standing upright — Christ's purity and authority amid the corruption
red tables being overturned — the disruption of commercial exploitation of worship
scattered objects on ground — the reversal of worldly order by divine judgment
figures cowering or fleeing — the power of righteous indignation
description: A simplified, modernist composition showing a figure in white standing among seated and crouching figures at red-draped tables. Objects appear scattered on the ground. The white-robed figure stands upright in the center while other figures in yellow, red, and dark clothing lean away or cower. At right, a figure appears to be lying or falling near overturned red fabric. The painting uses broad, flat areas of color with minimal detail, creating a scene of confrontation and disruption in a sparse, abstracted space.
theological_description: The cleansing of the temple is one of the few events recorded in all four Gospels, underscoring its central importance. Jesus enters the temple courts and finds merchants selling sacrificial animals and money changers conducting business. In righteous fury, he overturns their tables and drives them out, declaring: 'My house shall be called a house of prayer, but you make it a den of robbers' (Matthew 21:13). This prophetic action fulfills Malachi's prophecy that the Lord will suddenly come to his temple and purify it (Malachi 3:1-3). The simplified, modern style of this interpretation strips the scene to its essential confrontation: the lone figure in white — purity, holiness, divine authority — stands against the entrenched commerce that has corrupted sacred space. Jesus's action is not merely a reform of temple practice but a prophetic sign of the temple's coming destruction and replacement by his own body (John 2:19-21). The overturned tables represent the overturning of a religious system that had placed profit above prayer, access to God behind a paywall of sacrifice sales and currency exchange.

scripture_refs.reason: Jesus entering the temple, overturning tables, and declaring it should be a house of prayer not a den of robbers
John's account where Jesus makes a whip and drives out the merchants, and the disciples recall 'Zeal for your house will consume me'
The Lord suddenly coming to his temple to purify and refine — the prophetic background to Christ's action
Zeal for your house has consumed me — the psalm the disciples apply to Jesus's temple action
